Learning Objectives

Role of customer satisfaction in a company's marketing strategy
2
Build and interpret a Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)
3
Evaluate the financial case for retaining dissatisfied customers
4
Satisfaction–retention relationship and its moderating factors

Measure customer loyalty using the Customer Loyalty Index (CLI)
6
Calculate CLV — formula method and period-by-period PV method

LO1 Customer Satisfaction & Profitability
04 / 20
Customer Satisfaction Drives Profitability
Higher satisfaction → higher profit — but the relationship is non-linear
Key insight

Very Satisfied customers are disproportionately profitable — not just more profitable.

Chapter exclusive

Ch. 1 Fig 1-2: high-satisfaction firms' stock prices tripled over 10 years vs. flat for low-satisfaction firms.

LO2 Distributions vs. Averages

Would Removing Dissatisfied Customers Help?
Consider this scenario before moving to the next slide
The Scenario
What if the company had no dissatisfied customers?
Very Satisfied10%
Satisfied35%
Somewhat Satisfied55%
Somewhat Dissatisfied0%
Dissatisfied0%
Very Dissatisfied0%
Stop & Think

Before moving to the next slide — write down your prediction:

Would this company be better off under this scenario?

LO2
Gate 2 target
LO2 The Cognitive Trap — Revealed
07 / 20
Is the Company Better Off?
New distribution: VS 10% · S 35% · SS 55%
$250
Avg profit — base case
$178
Avg profit — no dissatisfied
−29%
Change in avg profit

The company is worse off. Removing unprofitable customers shifts the distribution toward mid-tier — not toward Very Satisfied. Averages hide what distributions reveal.

Also: CSI drops 72→65 and avg profit drops $250→$178. An 8-point CSI increase (72→80) would instead raise avg profit by ~31%. See Ch. 1 for this calculation.

LO3 Satisfaction & Retention
09 / 20
Customer Satisfaction & Retention

Satisfaction is positively related to retention — but moderating factors temper the relationship.

Local example

Nevada Power & Southwest Gas are natural monopolies — high retention despite potentially low satisfaction. Lack of competition weakens the link.

Chapter exclusive

Ch. 1 covers the zone of tolerance concept — see before Gate 2.

LO3LO4
Zone of tolerance — Ch. 1
LO3 Retention vs. Acquisition Economics
10 / 20
Retained Customers Cost Less Than New
80% retention scenario

$110 per retained vs. $500 per new customer — a 4.5× difference in marketing cost.

Chapter exclusive

Ch. 1 Fig. 1-8 also shows the 70% retention scenario — compare the two tables to see the profit leverage of a 10-point retention improvement.

LO3LO5
Fig. 1-8 — see 70% table in Ch. 1
LO4 Customer Loyalty
11 / 20
Retention ≠ Loyalty
Related concepts — but meaningfully different
+

Satisfaction → Retention

Higher satisfaction drives higher retention rates.

+

Retention → Loyalty

Higher retention supports building customer loyalty over time.

≠

Retention ≠ Loyalty

A customer can be retained without being loyal. Captive customers stay but would switch if they could.

LO4

12 / 20
Customer Loyalty Index (CLI)
5 dimensions averaged into a 0–100 loyalty score

Loyal · Repeat · Captive · New · Unprofitable

LO5 Customer Life Expectancy
14 / 20
Retention & Customer Life Expectancy

N = 1 ÷ (1 − r)

r = 1 − (1 ÷ N)

Paul Mitchell shampoo: 5 purchases → N=5, r=80%

LO5
LO6 Customer Lifetime Value
15 / 20
Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)
Present value of average profit per customer over their lifetime
LO6
LO6 CLV Equation Structure
16 / 20
Two Things to Notice About CLV
1 — Two terms, two strategic levers

The first term is the present value of customer profit over their lifetime. The second is acquisition cost. CLV is positive only if lifetime profit exceeds acquisition cost.

2 — Retention rate in numerator AND denominator

As r increases, the numerator rises AND the denominator falls. CLV therefore accelerates — it grows at an increasing rate as retention improves, not a linear rate.

LO6
LO6 CLV — Period-by-Period Method
17 / 20
CLV — Period-by-Period PV Method
Credit card example: 80% retention, 10% discount rate, AC=$51

CLV = $111.70
Sum of discounted cash flows at 10%

LO6
LO6 CLV — Practice Problem
18 / 20
Apply the CLV Formula
Internet Service Provider example
The Problem
1 million customers
Price = $40.00/month
Variable costs = $1.50/month
Loyalty program = $1.50/month
Retention rate = 78.8%/month
Interest rate = 1%/month
Acquisition cost = $0
What is the CLV? Try before next slide →
Reference formula:
LO6
LO6 CLV — Solution
19 / 20
CLV Practice Problem — Solution
VariableValueNote
Price$40.00/monthMonthly value
Variable costs$1.50 + $1.50 = $3.00/monthService + loyalty program
Margin (M)$40.00 − $3.00 = $37.00Contribution per customer
Retention rate (R)78.8% = 0.788Monthly
Interest rate (I)1% = 0.01Monthly — matches period
Acquisition cost (AC)$0Assumed
CLV$131.33Max justifiable AC = $131.33

If the company must spend to acquire customers, it should spend no more than $131.33. Acquisition cost above this makes CLV negative.

LO6
LO6 CLV Across Industries
20 / 20
CLV Varies Across Industries
Online customer NPV over 3 years · 10% discount rate
IndustryAcq. CostYr 1Yr 2Yr 33-Year CLV (NPV)
Electronics (online)−$50$10$12$11−$17.25
Groceries (online)−$90$15$60$57+$25.77
Apparel (online)−$50$15$60$74+$80.79

Electronics

Acquisition cost exceeds 3-year profit. Negative CLV — customer never fully pays back.

Groceries

High acquisition cost (~double others) but sustained repeat purchases recover it by year 2.

Apparel

Same acquisition cost as electronics but growing annual profit produces the highest CLV.

Original slide contained a 2012 screenshot. This table is an equivalent reconstruction from Ch. 1 Fig. 1-12. 10% discount rate applied.
